nativeRange.habitat: Trade cultivar collected from Central Vietnam (Gia Lai region commonly cited). Lineage variously attributed to the Rotala rotundifolia complex, Rotala wallichii, or an undescribed Rotala. The 'Rotala Vietnam' umbrella label encompasses this and adjacent collected forms.

decorPreferences: Nutrient-rich aquasoil substrate with iron-rich root tabs
High light (PAR 60+) for the signature orange-pink to burgundy-red top growth
CO2 injection (20-30 ppm) generally required for tight bushy form and saturated color
Soft acidic water tends to deepen the red coloration
Hedge-style trimming to maintain a dense carpet of colored tops
Lower nitrate levels promote stronger red/orange color expression

summary: Vietnamese trade cultivar of Rotala prized for its sunset orange-pink to burgundy-red top growth under high-tech conditions. Lineage is debated within the rotundifolia complex. A demanding background stem with iron-sensitive coloration.
careNotes:
Rotala 'H'Ra' is a Vietnamese-collected trade cultivar (Central Vietnam, Gia Lai region commonly cited) widely used in Dutch-style and competition aquascapes for its sunset-hued top growth, ranging from orange-pink through deep burgundy under strong light. Lineage is unsettled: some sources position it as a cultivar of Rotala rotundifolia, others align it with Rotala wallichii, and a few treat it as an undescribed Rotala from the rotundifolia complex. The 'Rotala Vietnam' umbrella term in the hobby trade is commonly applied to this cultivar and adjacent Vietnamese-origin Rotala forms; 'Rotala Gia Lai' is a related trade label.

Care is generally more demanding than common R. rotundifolia: best results tend to require high light (PAR 60+), reliable CO2 injection (20-30 ppm), iron-rich macro and micro dosing, and soft acidic water. Lower nitrate levels are widely reported to promote stronger red/orange coloration. Under low light or low CO2, color reverts to olive-green and the cultivar loses its distinguishing feature. The plant has a feathery, pine-needle-like texture with thin, pointed red or orange submerged leaves, and propagates via lateral shoots and stem cuttings, with a creeping growth habit via side shoots. Hedge-trim regularly to keep a tight carpet of colored tops near the back glass. Plants commonly arrive in either emersed or submerged form depending on season; fragile leaves require careful handling and acclimation to avoid melting.

DATA STATUS: Researched 2026-06-12. Primary source Aquarium Co-Op (user-verified URL) confirmed stem-plant traits, midground/background placement, high light, CO2 demand, sunset coloration, feathery texture, and creeping side-shoot propagation. Secondary source Buce Plant confirmed Central Vietnam origin, pH range, size, propagation, and the Lythraceae family, plus added the 'Rotala Gia Lai' alias and the low-nitrate-for-color note. Two sources concur on key care parameters; temperature range and detailed taxonomy still inherit from the rotundifolia-complex baseline.

primary.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Rotala H'Ra
primary.notes: Confirmed: stem plant, midground/background placement, high light, CO2 supplementation needed, creeping growth via side shoots, sunset orange/pink/red coloration under high light, feathery pine-needle-like texture, suited to high-tech planted tanks.

additional.name: Buce Plant — Rotala H'Ra
additional.notes: Confirmed: Central Vietnam origin, family Lythraceae, pH 6.5-7.5, 4-8 inch height, CO2 20-30 ppm, propagation via side shoots and axial roots. Added: 'Rotala Gia Lai' as alternative trade name, low-nitrate dosing strategy for color enhancement, note that fragile leaves need careful handling and plants may arrive emersed or submerged.
